chunks.title: Who is eligible for free legal aid (Section 12)
chunks.section: Who is eligible for free legal aid (Section 12)
chunks.text: Section 12 of the Legal Services Authorities Act, 1987 lists the persons entitled to free legal services. A person is eligible if they fall into any one of the following categories:

chunks.title: Who is eligible for free legal aid (Section 12)
chunks.section: Who is eligible for free legal aid (Section 12)

chunks.title: Who is eligible for free legal aid (Section 12)
chunks.section: Who is eligible for free legal aid (Section 12)
chunks.text:
Categories (a) to (g) are **income-blind**: a person in these groups qualifies regardless of how much they earn. The income test in clause (h) only decides eligibility when none of the categorical grounds apply.
Some States and NALSA schemes extend indicative support to senior citizens and transgender persons; these are covered by State-specific schemes rather than the fixed Section 12 categories, so confirm the current scheme with the State Legal Services Authority.
Eligibility is a screening question only. Under Section 13, legal services may be granted where the person has a prima facie case to prosecute or defend, and the Legal Services Authority makes the final decision.

chunks.title: Income ceilings for legal aid (Section 12(h))
chunks.text:
When a person does not fall into any of the categorical grounds in Section 12(a)–(g), eligibility turns on annual income. Section 12(h) sets an income ceiling; a person whose total annual income is at or below the ceiling qualifies for free legal services.
The ceiling is fixed separately by each State Government for cases within the State, and by the Central Government for cases before the Supreme Court through the Supreme Court Legal Services Committee (SCLSC). Because States revise these figures over time, the numbers below are indicative starter values only:

chunks.title: Income ceilings for legal aid (Section 12(h))
chunks.text:
- Cases before the Supreme Court (SCLSC): a higher ceiling, of the order of Rs 9,00,000 per year.
- Delhi, Karnataka, Uttar Pradesh, West Bengal: lower state ceilings, of the order of Rs 1,00,000 per year.
- Rajasthan: of the order of Rs 1,50,000 per year.
- Maharashtra, Tamil Nadu: of the order of Rs 3,00,000 per year.
- A common default used when a specific State limit is not known: Rs 3,00,000 per year.
To prove income, applicants generally provide an income certificate, a BPL card or ration card, or a self-declaration/affidavit of income. The person only needs to establish that their income does not exceed the applicable ceiling. If the income is above the ceiling and no categorical ground applies, the person may still apply, and the authority can consider special circumstances.

chunks.title: How to apply for free legal aid
chunks.section: How to apply for free legal aid
chunks.text:
Legal services are delivered through a hierarchy of institutions: the National Legal Services Authority (NALSA) at the top, State Legal Services Authorities (SLSA) in each State, High Court Legal Services Committees (HCLSC), District Legal Services Authorities (DLSA) in each district, and Taluk/Sub-divisional Legal Services Committees (TLSC) at the taluk level. For Supreme Court matters, the Supreme Court Legal Services Committee (SCLSC) is the point of contact.
How a person applies:

chunks.title: How to apply for free legal aid
chunks.section: How to apply for free legal aid
chunks.text:
1. Approach the nearest legal services institution — most commonly the DLSA, which usually sits in the district court complex. You can also apply to the SLSA, TLSC, or SCLSC depending on where the case is.
2. Apply online at nalsa.gov.in, or through the NALSA Legal Services Mobile App.
3. Call the national legal aid helpline 15100 for guidance.
4. Fill the prescribed application form. A Para-Legal Volunteer (PLV) at the institution helps applicants fill the form free of charge and explains the process.
The application asks for the applicant's identity, address, the category of eligibility relied on, income (if relying on the income ground), and a short description of the legal problem. Supporting documents are attached to prove identity, address, and the ground of eligibility.

chunks.title: How to apply for free legal aid
chunks.section: How to apply for free legal aid
chunks.text: After the application is submitted, the Legal Services Authority scrutinises it, checks eligibility under Section 12 and whether there is a prima facie case under Section 13, and — if granted — assigns a panel lawyer to the applicant at no cost. There is no court fee or lawyer's fee for an aided person.

chunks.title: What free legal services include
chunks.text:
Free legal services for an eligible person are not limited to a lawyer in court. They typically include:
- Payment of court fees, process fees, and all other charges payable in connection with the legal proceedings.
- The services of a panel lawyer to represent the person in legal proceedings.
- Drafting of legal documents, petitions, appeals, and paperwork connected to the case.
- Obtaining and supplying certified copies of orders, judgments, and other documents in the proceedings.
- Preparation of paper books, including printing and translation of documents, where required.
- Legal advice on any matter, and assistance in pre-litigation stages such as drafting notices.

chunks.title: What free legal services include
chunks.text:
Legal services also cover mediation and conciliation, help before tribunals and authorities, not only regular courts, and legal awareness. Front Offices at legal services institutions receive walk-in requests and route them.
Quality safeguards apply: panel lawyers are selected on experience, and an aided person may request a change of lawyer for good reason. The person should be kept informed about the progress of their case.
Free legal services can be withdrawn if it is found that the person obtained aid by misrepresentation or fraud, if the person has adequate means, or if the person does not cooperate. Withdrawal is an exception and is decided by the Authority.

chunks.title: NALSA schemes for special groups
chunks.text: Beyond the general Section 12 categories, NALSA runs targeted schemes to reach vulnerable groups. These commonly include:

chunks.title: NALSA schemes for special groups
chunks.text:
- Victims of trafficking and commercial sexual exploitation — legal aid, rescue-and-rehabilitation support, and victim compensation guidance.
- Child-friendly legal services — support for children in conflict with law and child victims, coordinated with Juvenile Justice institutions and Child Welfare Committees.
- Scheme for effective implementation of the Scheduled Castes and Scheduled Tribes (Prevention of Atrocities) Act — legal aid and awareness for SC/ST atrocity victims.
- Legal services to persons with mental illness and mental disabilities.
- Legal services for senior citizens — assistance under State schemes for maintenance and welfare.
- Legal services to victims of acid attacks — help with compensation and treatment-related claims.
- Protection and enforcement of the rights of workers in the unorganised sector, and of migrant labour.
- Legal services to disaster victims and to persons in custody, including under-trial review.

chunks.title: NALSA schemes for special groups
chunks.text:
Each scheme identifies the target beneficiaries, the kind of help offered (legal aid, awareness, victim compensation, follow-up), and the delivery mechanism through DLSAs and Para-Legal Volunteers. A person who belongs to one of these groups should mention it when applying, because it can establish eligibility and route them to the right support.
Victim compensation is separate from legal aid: under Section 357A of the Code of Criminal Procedure and State victim compensation schemes, victims of certain crimes may claim compensation, and legal services institutions can assist with those claims.

chunks.title: Lok Adalats, mediation, and documents to carry
chunks.section: Lok Adalats, mediation, and documents to carry
chunks.text:
## Lok Adalat and amicable settlement
Lok Adalats are organised by legal services institutions under Sections 19–22 of the Act to settle disputes amicably. A Lok Adalat can take up pending court cases and pre-litigation disputes referred to it. Its award has the status of a decree of a civil court and is final and binding on the parties, with no appeal. There is no court fee for matters settled in a Lok Adalat, and court fee already paid may be refunded. Permanent Lok Adalats handle certain public-utility service disputes. Mediation and conciliation are also offered before matters escalate to litigation.
## Documents commonly required
When applying for legal aid, an applicant generally carries:

chunks.title: Lok Adalats, mediation, and documents to carry
chunks.section: Lok Adalats, mediation, and documents to carry
chunks.text:
- The completed Legal Services Authority application form.
- Proof of identity — Aadhaar, Voter ID, Passport, or Driving Licence.
- Proof of residence or address, to establish which DLSA has jurisdiction.
- Two recent passport-size photographs.
- A self-declaration or affidavit of the facts and of eligibility.
Depending on the ground of eligibility, additional proof is needed: a caste certificate for SC/ST, a disability (UDID) certificate, an FIR or official certificate for disaster or trafficking victims, employment proof for an industrial workman, a custody reference for a person in custody, age proof for a child or senior citizen, and an income certificate, BPL card, or ration card for income-based eligibility.

chunks.title: Lok Adalats, mediation, and documents to carry
chunks.section: Lok Adalats, mediation, and documents to carry
chunks.text: Matter-specific documents help the panel lawyer prepare — for example, a copy of the FIR or charge-sheet in a criminal case, a marriage certificate and any protection-order copies in a family case, or title deeds and tax receipts in a property dispute. Applicants should carry originals plus one photocopy; a Para-Legal Volunteer can help certify copies.
